Andthen And then And then Andthen [Music] my premature death the English oligopoly your hired assassin killed me These were the words spoken by Napoleon Bonaparte in his last will and testament in April 1821. Bonaparte, one of the most talented strategists in history, little knew that this phrase and other events would awaken people's imagination regarding his end. The day after his death, still under mechanical custody on May 5, 1821, 16 observers attended the autopsy, including seven doctors. They were unanimous in their conclusion: Napoleon had died of stomach cancer. However, the doubts that Napoleon himself harbored about what really happened will never completely disappear. Did the British government hasten his death? Did his French rivals poison his wine? Was it really Napoleon who died amidst the chaos in May 1821? Hello, I'm Leandro Lusa, and today's video will talk about the possible poisoning of Napoleon and his consequent death. If you like the video, don't forget to leave a like and subscribe to the channel. [Music] Napoleon was born in 1769 into a modest Italian family in Corsica, a Mediterranean island that had passed into French hands a year before his birth. [Music] By 1811, Bonaparte already ruled 70 million people and dominated much of Europe. Four years later, his dynastic, political, imperial, and military dreams were shattered, and he was exiled to the remote island of Saint Helena in the south-central Atlantic Ocean. There, he and his family lived in a village called Long House until the death of the former Emperor of France. For months before his death, Napoleon suffered from abdominal pain, nausea, night sweats, and fever. When he wasn't constipated, he had diarrhea and lost a lot of weight. He constantly complained of headaches, weak legs, and discomfort in bright light. His speech became confused, and at this point, his gums, lips, and nails were colorless. It then occurred to him that he was being poisoned, but probably because of his mental confusion caused by the illness.  He decides he has the same disease that killed his father, stomach cancer, and that all medical help is useless. [Music] On May 14, 1821, he lost consciousness, and the following day the news that the great man had died caused a worldwide shock, and questions began to arise. The first person to create a theory about Bonaparte's death was the one who would become his personal physician, the Irishman Berry or Miriam, who had been a surgeon on the HMS Bellerophon, a ship that picked up Napoleon when he surrendered after the Battle of Waterloo. Miriam cared for the former emperor for three years until she made the explosive statement that the British governor of Saint Helena was a rude, damned, and insidious man who had intended to shorten Napoleon's life, or rather, was dismissed after that. He was the perfect subject to play the role of the British villain, which is the version that went down in history, and not by chance, the version that Napoleon probably wanted the world to believe. Napoleon had a very cunning plan to escape Saint Helena. It is legal for that.  The local climate was fatally weakening, and to that end, he used the medical authority of Doctor Almir, as Amparo or Mira supported his claims. In 1818, he accused the governor of trying to hasten Napoleon's death, and in 1822 he published a book in which he stated that the British government was determined to eliminate any possibility of another Napoleonic return. Many people suspected that he was right, mainly because at the time arsenic was widely used to kill people, as there was still no method to identify the presence of the poison in a corpse. So, about a hundred years passed until the story was picked up from where it had left off, and how many private diaries of Napoleon's servant were published in the 1950s, offering intimate accounts of the Emperor's last days. The Swedish dentist, without fortfood, believed he had found irrefutable proof of one of the 31 symptoms of arsenic poisoning discovered by scientists since 1821. Napoleon presented 28 of them, so he asked a Scottish university to perform a newly invented arsenic detection test, an analysis called  Neutron activation tests performed on Napoleon's hair, dated 1816, 1817, and 1818, revealed fatally high levels of arsenic in his system. The theory of Almir, the former emperor's personal physician, seemed to be correct. Then Canadian millionaire Ben Kweller reached the same conclusion using a different method. Convinced that Napoleon had been assassinated, he reviewed written memoirs from those who lived in the Long House, searching for clues. He collected evidence related to the symptoms described in these memoirs. Based on this and the peaks of arsenic absorption shown by the new chemical analysis, they believed they possessed evidence that the poisonous substance had been administered to the emperor at regular intervals over several years. They further argued that the potassium tartrate used in his will prevented Napoleon's stomach from expelling the toxic compound, and that these were symptoms of poisoning. The book "The Leader Installed Murders on St. Helena" also mentioned the new suspect, the former friend. Napoleon Charles tested the Marquis de Montholon, and according to his book, he had two main motives for assassinating Bonaparte: he was desperate to leave the island, and his wife had been seduced by Napoleon. Furthermore, the restored Bourbon kings in France, who had as much interest as the British in keeping Napoleon under control, had threatened, according to the leader and matchmaker, to make public Montholon's diversion of military funds if he did not agree to poison Napoleon with arsenic. However, this elaborate theory did not convince everyone. Even if arsenic was the cause of Napoleon's death, it did not mean that someone had killed the French Emperor with this substance. In the 1980s, the debate about the poisoning took a different direction. Napoleon could simply have absorbed the arsenic from his environment. Any 19th-century house was saturated with arsenic: cosmetics, hair tonic, cigarettes, sealing wax, cookware, insect repellent, rat poison, cake frosting— almost everything contained arsenic. When a chemist from Newcastle University conducted a test with a piece of paper...  A coffin from a long wall, stolen by a tourist in the 19th century, revealed that poisonous gases emanating from a mold growing behind it could have contributed to Napoleon's fatal decline. Researchers compared the hair of Napoleon's son with his first wife, Empress Josephine, to that of ten living people and concluded that Europeans in the early 19th century had up to 100 times more arsenic in their bodies than a living person today. However, those convinced that Napoleon was assassinated did not accept this hypothesis for several years. The two schools of thought fought with evidence and counter-evidence. The FBI, the EAD Florence Institute of Strasbourg, laboratories, and the Paris police all conducted tests and all confirmed the findings of arsenic in Napoleon's system. However, neither could definitively establish how the poison ended up there, until French authorities allowed the coffin to be opened to exhume the body and collect better evidence about Napoleon's end. Theories about the final fate of one of history's most fascinating figures persisted.  These topics will continue to be debated, and you told me in the comments, "What do you think happened?" Something about Napoleon Bonaparte is on the screen. I'll leave it at that for now. I leave you with my sincerest thanks, a big hug, and until the next video. [Music]

próprias para o consumo humano como água mineral de garrafinha a diferença entre a água que compramos no mercado e água do mar é a concentração desses compostos e e enquanto que na água engarrafada adequada para ingestão a concentração de sais minerais como sódio potássio bromo cálcio eo magnésio abaixa na água do Oceano só concentração é bem mais elevada o que a deixa imprópria para o consumo humano Oi e a de salinização que permite que essa água salgada dos oceanos possa ser consumida sem maiores problemas é a de salinização consiste em um processo ou um conjunto de processos capazes de retirar o excesso de sal e outros minerais da água e os dois principais tipos de dessalinização são as de salinização térmica EA de salinização por osmose reversa e a ver salinização térmica é a forma mais antiga de uma forma bastante simplificada ela consiste em ferver a água capturar o vapor e condensado novamente em Água desta vez Pura Sem sais basicamente replicando o efeito do Sol sobre as águas superficiais do planeta já de salinização por osmose reversa foi criada nos anos 60 e hoje parece ser o processo mais promissor e que tem de tudo para dominar o mercado uma das principais diferenças entre os dois processos é que é osmose reversa não usa calor esse método não serve água e a explicar de uma forma bem simples essa técnica funciona aplicando uma grande quantidade de pressão na água e forçando ela contra uma membrana para que a água passe essa membrana ela precisa deixar para trás as moléculas de sal como cloreto de sódio e é assim de um lado temos a água sem sal e do outro que restou da água agora muito mais salgada do que no início já que ela ficou com todos ao que foi separado no processo e atualmente a maioria das cerca de 16 mil plantas de dessalinização em operação no planeta usando tecnologias de osmose reversa contudo Esse é um processo caro principalmente pelo custo de energia assim quem acaba utilizando esse processo são países onde a água é escassa e logo cara e a energia abundante e logo barata e não é à toa que a grande maioria dos processos de dessalinização acontecem em regiões como Oriente Médio eo norte da África regiões ricas em
